title.emphasis: Part I: The Subject on Trial: A Legal and Scientific Refutation of the Autonomous Individual

section.para: The entire intellectual edifice of Western humanism and its political offshoots rests upon a single, load-bearing pillar: the concept of the autonomous individual. This idealized subject—rational, self-sufficient, and distinct from nature—is the protagonist of Enlightenment philosophy, the basis for modern rights discourse, and the presumed agent of economic and political life. Before condemning the ideologies built upon this foundation, it is necessary to first prosecute the foundation itself. This section will demonstrate that this liberal subject is a legal fiction, a biological fantasy, and a psychological fallacy. By establishing this at the outset, the subsequent condemnations become direct applications of this foundational refutation. The evidence shows that the autonomous individual is not a natural fact to be discovered but a social reality constructed through legal and political power, a construction whose claims to truth collapse under the weight of scientific scrutiny.

title.emphasis: The Legal Fiction of Autonomy and Personhood

section.para: Western legal tradition has constructed and enshrined a narrow, exclusionary definition of "personhood" that serves specific economic and colonial interests. This legal construct is the primary tool for building the normative cage, as it establishes a rigid hierarchy of being and codifies a set of assumptions about who qualifies for rights and recognition. An examination of foundational human rights documents, decolonial legal theory, and the legal status of non-human entities reveals that "personhood" has never been a biological fact but has always been a flexible, political instrument.

The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR), the foundational document of the modern rights regime, proclaims a vision of universal dignity for all "members of the human family".1 Yet, a critical analysis of its text reveals that the "universal" human at its core is a specific and exclusionary construct. Despite being drafted in the shadow of a global conflict that included the systematic murder of hundreds of thousands of disabled people, disability is conspicuously absent from the UDHR's list of protected categories in Article 2.2 This is not a mere oversight but is indicative of a deeper, structural ableism. The document's protagonist is an abstract, disembodied, and implicitly able-bodied human being, establishing an able-bodied norm at the very heart of modern rights discourse.2

This process of legal definition is not neutral; it is a technology of power. Decolonial legal theory posits that the modern world is shaped by a "double fracture": an environmental fracture separating humanity from nature, and a colonial fracture separating the colonizer from the colonized.2 These are not parallel processes but a single, integrated logic, legally codified in the rigid binary between "person" and "property." A person is a subject with rights, while property is an object to be owned and used. This legal technology is the mechanism that historically enabled both humans (slaves) and land to be converted into capital, stripping them of their agency and subjectivity.2 The mindset that justified the objectification and extraction of resources from colonized peoples was the same mindset that objectified and extracted from the natural world.2 The legal definition of the "human person" was thus constructed in explicit opposition to a de-animated "nature" and a dehumanized "other," a process that laid the groundwork for both colonial exploitation and planetary-scale ecological devastation.

The ultimate perversion of this legal logic is the doctrine of corporate personhood. Originating in medieval law and solidifying in the 19th-century United States, this legal fiction grants artificial, profit-driven entities the rights of a "person" under the law.2 This creates a legal landscape where a non-living, extractive entity designed for capital accumulation is afforded more legal protection and standing than the living ecosystems it destroys.2 The granting of personhood to corporations while denying it to nature is a stark illustration of how colonial-capitalist values are embedded within the very structure of modern law. It demonstrates conclusively that legal personhood is not a recognition of inherent biological or moral status, but a flexible political tool conferred to serve the interests of power.

In direct opposition to this tradition, the contemporary Rights of Nature movement represents a legal counter-paradigm. Following the lead of Indigenous legal traditions, which are founded on relational ontologies, countries like Ecuador and Bolivia have amended their constitutions to recognize Nature, or Pacha Mama, as a legal subject with inherent rights to exist, persist, and regenerate.2 These legal innovations fundamentally challenge the anthropocentric and property-based premises of Western law, demonstrating that the legal construction of personhood can be re-imagined and expanded beyond the narrow confines of the normative human subject.2

The concept of the self-sufficient, autonomous individual is not only a legal construct but also a biological fantasy. Scientific evidence from a wide range of domains—from microbiology and epigenetics to disability studies—converges on a single, powerful conclusion: life is not characterized by independence but by a profound and inescapable interdependence. The ideologies of egotism and libertarianism, which are predicated on the myth of the rugged individual, are therefore not merely socially undesirable; they are scientifically illiterate.

The most incisive critique of the autonomous individual comes from the field of disability justice, which refutes what it terms the "myth of independence".2 This myth, a cornerstone of Western rights discourse, constructs a fictive subject who stands alone and is capable of enforcing their own rights.2 Disability justice, in contrast, champions the principle of interdependence, recognizing the fundamental truth that all beings are mutually reliant on complex networks of care for their survival and flourishing.2 This is not an aspirational ideal but a material reality. The myth of independence serves capitalist and ableist logics by individualizing responsibility and casting those with visible support needs as "burdens" rather than as inherent parts of the human condition.2 By reframing interdependence as the baseline, disability justice exposes the self-sufficient individual as a fiction sustained only by the unacknowledged and often exploited labor of others.

This social reality is a direct reflection of a deeper biological truth. The individual organism is not a sovereign, self-contained entity. The human body itself is a complex ecosystem, a symbiotic collective. The trillions of microorganisms residing in the gastrointestinal tract, the gut microbiome, are now recognized as a key player in a bidirectional communication system with the central nervous system, known as the gut-brain axis.5 This internal ecosystem influences mood, cognition, and behavior. For instance, up to 95% of the body's serotonin, a key mood regulator, is produced in the gut, a process heavily influenced by resident microbes.5 This provides a hard scientific basis for the concept of a porous, non-sovereign self. Even our most intimate states of mind are not wholly our own but are co-regulated by a vast internal community of non-human others.5

This biological interconnectedness extends across generations. The science of epigenetics has demonstrated that profound environmental factors, including stress and trauma, can cause heritable changes in gene expression without altering the DNA sequence itself.2 Research has shown that the traumas of colonialism, slavery, and systemic racism can leave biological "scars" that are passed down through generations, contributing to health disparities and cycles of intergenerational trauma.2 This refutes the idea of the individual as a "blank slate" or an isolated agent making choices in a vacuum. We are biologically constituted by the lived experiences of our ancestors, our very physiology shaped by a history of collective suffering and resilience. The libertarian fantasy of the atomized individual, free from unchosen obligations, collapses in the face of this biological reality of inherited responsibility.

title.emphasis: The Malleable Self: Neurological and Psychological Evidence

section.para: The final pillar supporting the normative subject is the assumption of a stable, rational self—a consistent "I" that persists through time, making decisions based on a fixed set of values and a clear perception of reality. Overwhelming evidence from social psychology and modern neuroscience demonstrates that this, too, is a fallacy. The self is not a static essence to be discovered but a malleable, situational construct, profoundly dependent on its social and biological environment. This scientific reality invalidates any ideology predicated on a fixed or universal "human nature."

The most dramatic evidence for the malleability of the self comes from the study of coercive persuasion, or "thought reform." Research based on the experiences of prisoners of war and members of high-control groups demonstrates that a totalistic environment—one that controls all information and social contact—can systematically dismantle and reconstruct an individual's identity, beliefs, and behaviors.5 This process is not a mystical "brainwashing" but a methodical application of psychological principles. A key engine of this change is the manipulation of cognitive dissonance, the profound psychological discomfort that occurs when one's behavior contradicts one's beliefs.6 By compelling an individual to behave in a manner consistent with a new ideology, the coercive system creates an unbearable internal conflict. Within the closed, high-pressure environment, the only psychological escape is to adopt the new beliefs that make the behavior seem rational. Action precedes and coerces belief.6 This proves that even our most deeply held convictions are not immutable but are contingent upon the social reality in which we are embedded.

The failure of the CIA's infamous Project MKUltra serves as a crucial negative case study that reinforces this conclusion. For two decades, the project attempted to achieve mind control through direct, brute-force methods such as the covert administration of high-dose psychedelics, electroshock therapy, and psychological torture.6 The project was a categorical failure. It demonstrated that while it is possible to destroy a human mind, it is not possible to predictably control it or "open the wiped-away mind to control by an outsider".6 The project's fundamental flaw was its premise: the belief that the mind is a simple machine to be reprogrammed. The failure of this "hardware" approach, contrasted with the relative success of the "software" approach of environmental manipulation used in thought reform, proves the decisive power of the social construction of reality in shaping the self.6

Modern neuroscience provides the biological mechanism for this malleability. The brain is not a fixed, static organ but is characterized by neuroplasticity—the ability to reorganize itself by forming new neural connections in response to experience.5 Learning, trauma, and even conscious practice can reshape the physical structure of the brain. This dynamism is further highlighted by the development of brain-computer interfaces (BCIs), which aim to create a direct, high-bandwidth connection between the mind and external computational systems.7 The emerging field of "digital twins" of consciousness, in which AI is used to create computational models that can predict the activity of neural circuits, further reinforces the idea that the self is an information pattern—a complex, computable, and therefore potentially modifiable, process.8

The legal, biological, and psychological critiques are not parallel arguments; they are a single, unified refutation. The legal framework's construction of an autonomous individual is not an abstract error; it is a direct contradiction of biological reality. By enshrining the "myth of independence" in law, the system actively generates the social conditions—such as the atomization of individuals and the devaluation of communal care—that scientific evidence from social psychology demonstrates are precursors to profound psychological harm and manipulation.2 The legal system constructs a social reality based on a biological falsehood. This false reality of radical individualism isolates people, which in turn makes them psychologically vulnerable to the very forms of control and exploitation that the legal system is supposed to prevent. The ideologies under condemnation in this report are both a product of this flawed legal architecture and a primary justification for its perpetuation. A coherent strategy for liberation must therefore simultaneously attack the legal fictions
and build social structures that reflect the biological reality of interdependence.

row.entry: **Legally Constructed & Biologically Interdependent:** Legally, "personhood" is a flexible political tool, not a biological fact.2 Biologically, all life exists in a state of profound interdependence, from the symbiotic gut-brain axis to societal networks of care.2

**Malleable & Situationally Dependent:** The self is a dynamic construct, profoundly shaped by its social environment, as demonstrated by coercive persuasion research.6 The brain's neuroplasticity allows for continuous physical reorganization in response to experience.5

**Ecologically Embedded & Co-constitutive:** Decolonial legal theory and the Rights of Nature movement reframe the human as an integrated part of a living ecological community, not its master.2 The legal person/property binary is a colonial artifice.2

**Biologically Constituted by History:** The science of epigenetics demonstrates that the biological impacts of ancestral trauma and environmental stress can be heritably transmitted across generations, refuting the idea of the individual as a discrete, ahistorical entity.2
